{\n\t\tlogger.V(4).Info(\"Container has pinned cpus\", \"podName\", pod.Name, \"containerName\", container.Name)\n\t\treturn true\n\t}\n\treturn false\n}\n\n// parsePercentage parses the percentage string to numeric value.\nfunc parsePercentage(v string) (int64, error) {\n\tif !strings.HasSuffix(v, \"%\") {\n\t\treturn 0, fmt.Errorf(\"percentage expected, got '%s'\", v)\n\t}\n\tpercentage, err := strconv.ParseInt(strings.TrimRight(v, \"%\"), 10, 0)\n\tif err != nil {\n\t\treturn 0, fmt.Errorf(\"invalid number in percentage '%s'\", v)\n\t}\n\tif percentage < 0 || percentage > 100 {\n\t\treturn 0, fmt.Errorf(\"percentage must be between 0 and 100\")\n\t}\n\treturn percentage, nil\n}\n\n// ParseQOSReserved parses the --qos-reserved option\nfunc ParseQOSReserved(m map[string]string) (*map[v1.ResourceName]int64, error) {\n\treservations := make(map[v1.ResourceName]int64)\n\tfor k, v := range m {\n\t\tswitch v1.ResourceName(k) {\n\t\t// Only memory resources are supported.\n\t\tcase v1.ResourceMemory:\n\t\t\tq, err := parsePercentage(v)\n\t\t\tif err != nil {\n\t\t\t\treturn nil, fmt.Errorf(\"failed to parse percentage %q for %q resource: %w\", v, k, err)\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t\treservations[v1.ResourceName(k)] = q\n\t\tdefault:\n\t\t\treturn nil, fmt.Errorf(\"cannot reserve %q resource\", k)\n\t\t}\n\t}\n\treturn &reservations, nil\n}\n\nfunc containerDevicesFromResourceDeviceInstances(devs devicemanager.ResourceDeviceInstances) []*podresourcesapi.ContainerDevices {\n\tvar respDevs []*podresourcesapi.ContainerDevices\n\n\tfor resourceName, resourceDevs := range devs {\n\t\tfor devID, dev := range resourceDevs {\n\t\t\ttopo := dev.GetTopology()\n\t\t\tif topo == nil {\n\t\t\t\t// Some device plugin do not report the topology information.\n\t\t\t\t// This is legal, so we report the devices anyway,\n\t\t\t\t// let the client decide what to do.\n\t\t\t\trespDevs = append(respDevs, &podresourcesapi.ContainerDevices{\n\t\t\t\t\tResourceName: resourceName,\n\t\t\t\t\tDeviceIds:    []string{devID},\n\t\t\t\t})\n\t\t\t\tcontinue\n\t\t\t}\n\n\t\t\tfor _, node := range topo.GetNodes() {\n\t\t\t\trespDevs = append(respDevs, &podresourcesapi.ContainerDevices{\n\t\t\t\t\tResourceName: resourceName,\n\t\t\t\t\tDeviceIds:    []string{devID},\n\t\t\t\t\tTopology: &podresourcesapi.TopologyInfo{\n\t\t\t\t\t\tNodes: []*podresourcesapi.NUMANode{\n\t\t\t\t\t\t\t{\n\t\t\t\t\t\t\t\tID: node.GetID(),\n\t\t\t\t\t\t\t},\n\t\t\t\t\t\t},\n\t\t\t\t\t},\n\t\t\t\t})\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t}\n\t}\n\n\treturn respDevs\n}\n", "numLines": 320, "startLine": 1, "totalLines": 320}}

    17→package cm
    18→
    19→import (
    20→	v1 "k8s.io/api/core/v1"

    22→	"k8s.io/klog/v2"
    23→	"k8s.io/utils/cpuset"
    24→)
    25→
    26→// ResourceConfig holds information about all the supported cgroup resource parameters.
    27→type ResourceConfig struct {
    28→	// Memory limit (in bytes).
    29→	Memory *int64
    30→	// CPU set (number of CPUs the cgroup has access to).
    31→	CPUSet cpuset.CPUSet
    32→	// CPU shares (relative weight vs. other containers).
    33→	CPUShares *uint64
    34→	// CPU hardcap limit (in usecs). Allowed cpu time in a given period.
    35→	CPUQuota *int64
    36→	// CPU quota period.
    37→	CPUPeriod *uint64
    38→	// HugePageLimit map from page size (in bytes) to limit (in bytes)
    39→	HugePageLimit map[int64]int64
    40→	// Maximum number of pids
    41→	PidsLimit *int64
    42→	// Unified for cgroup v2
    43→	Unified map[string]string
    44→}
    45→
    46→// CgroupName is the abstract name of a cgroup prior to any driver specific conversion.
    47→// It is specified as a list of strings from its individual components, such as:
    48→// {"kubepods", "burstable", "pod1234-abcd-5678-efgh"}
    49→type CgroupName []string
    50→
    51→// CgroupConfig holds the cgroup configuration information.
    52→// This is common object which is used to specify
    53→// cgroup information to both systemd and raw cgroup fs
    54→// implementation of the Cgroup Manager interface.
    55→type CgroupConfig struct {
    56→	// Fully qualified name prior to any driver specific conversions.
    57→	Name CgroupName
    58→	// ResourceParameters contains various cgroups settings to apply.
    59→	ResourceParameters *ResourceConfig
    60→}
    61→
    62→// CgroupManager allows for cgroup management.
    63→// Supports Cgroup Creation ,Deletion and Updates.
    64→type CgroupManager interface {
    65→	// Create creates and applies the cgroup configurations on the cgroup.
    66→	// It just creates the leaf cgroups.
    67→	// It expects the parent cgroup to already exist.
    68→	Create(klog.Logger, *CgroupConfig) error
    69→	// Destroy the cgroup.
    70→	Destroy(klog.Logger, *CgroupConfig) error
    71→	// Update cgroup configuration.
    72→	Update(klog.Logger, *CgroupConfig) error
    73→	// Validate checks if the cgroup is valid
    74→	Validate(name CgroupName) error
    75→	// Exists checks if the cgroup already exists
    76→	Exists(name CgroupName) bool
    77→	// Name returns the literal cgroupfs name on the host after any driver specific conversions.
    78→	// We would expect systemd implementation to make appropriate name conversion.
    79→	// For example, if we pass {"foo", "bar"}
    80→	// then systemd should convert the name to something like
    81→	// foo.slice/foo-bar.slice
    82→	Name(name CgroupName) string
    83→	// CgroupName converts the literal cgroupfs name on the host to an internal identifier.
    84→	CgroupName(name string) CgroupName
    85→	// Pids scans through all subsystems to find pids associated with specified cgroup.
    86→	Pids(logger klog.Logger, name CgroupName) []int
    87→	// ReduceCPULimits reduces the CPU CFS values to the minimum amount of shares.
    88→	ReduceCPULimits(logger klog.Logger, cgroupName CgroupName) error
    89→	// MemoryUsage returns current memory usage of the specified cgroup, as read from the cgroupfs.
    90→	MemoryUsage(name CgroupName) (int64, error)
    91→	// Get the resource config values applied to the cgroup for specified resource type
    92→	GetCgroupConfig(name CgroupName, resource v1.ResourceName) (*ResourceConfig, error)
    93→	// Set resource config for the specified resource type on the cgroup
    94→	SetCgroupConfig(logger klog.Logger, name CgroupName, resourceConfig *ResourceConfig) error
    95→	// Version of the cgroup implementation on the host
    96→	Version() int
    97→}
    98→
    99→// QOSContainersInfo stores the names of containers per qos
   100→type QOSContainersInfo struct {
   101→	Guaranteed CgroupName
   102→	BestEffort CgroupName
   103→	Burstable  CgroupName
   104→}
   105→
   106→// PodContainerManager stores and manages pod level containers
   107→// The Pod workers interact with the PodContainerManager to create and destroy
   108→// containers for the pod.
   109→type PodContainerManager interface {
   110→	// GetPodContainerName returns the CgroupName identifier, and its literal cgroupfs form on the host.
   111→	GetPodContainerName(*v1.Pod) (CgroupName, string)
   112→
   113→	// EnsureExists takes a pod as argument and makes sure that
   114→	// pod cgroup exists if qos cgroup hierarchy flag is enabled.
   115→	// If the pod cgroup doesn't already exist this method creates it.
   116→	EnsureExists(logger klog.Logger, pod *v1.Pod) error
   117→
   118→	// Exists returns true if the pod cgroup exists.
   119→	Exists(*v1.Pod) bool
   120→
   121→	// Destroy takes a pod Cgroup name as argument and destroys the pod's container.
   122→	Destroy(logger klog.Logger, name CgroupName) error
   123→
   124→	// ReduceCPULimits reduces the CPU CFS values to the minimum amount of shares.
   125→	ReduceCPULimits(logger klog.Logger, name CgroupName) error
   126→
   127→	// GetAllPodsFromCgroups enumerates the set of pod uids to their associated cgroup based on state of cgroupfs system.
   128→	GetAllPodsFromCgroups() (map[types.UID]CgroupName, error)
   129→
   130→	// IsPodCgroup returns true if the literal cgroupfs name corresponds to a pod
   131→	IsPodCgroup(cgroupfs string) (bool, types.UID)
   132→
   133→	// Get value of memory usage for the pod Cgroup
   134→	GetPodCgroupMemoryUsage(pod *v1.Pod) (uint64, error)
   135→
   136→	// Get the resource config values applied to the pod cgroup for specified resource type
   137→	GetPodCgroupConfig(pod *v1.Pod, resource v1.ResourceName) (*ResourceConfig, error)
   138→
   139→	// Set resource config values for the specified resource type on the pod cgroup
   140→	SetPodCgroupConfig(logger klog.Logger, pod *v1.Pod, resourceConfig *ResourceConfig) error
   141→}
   142→

    17→package cm
    18→
    19→import (
    20→	v1 "k8s.io/api/core/v1"
    21→	runtimeapi "k8s.io/cri-api/pkg/apis/runtime/v1"
    22→	"k8s.io/klog/v2"
    23→	"k8s.io/kubernetes/pkg/kubelet/cm/cpumanager"
    24→	"k8s.io/kubernetes/pkg/kubelet/cm/memorymanager"
    25→	"k8s.io/kubernetes/pkg/kubelet/cm/topologymanager"
    26→)
    27→
    28→type InternalContainerLifecycle interface {
    29→	PreCreateContainer(logger klog.Logger, pod *v1.Pod, container *v1.Container, containerConfig *runtimeapi.ContainerConfig) error
    30→	PreStartContainer(logger klog.Logger, pod *v1.Pod, container *v1.Container, containerID string) error
    31→	PostStopContainer(logger klog.Logger, containerID string) error
    32→}
    33→
    34→// Implements InternalContainerLifecycle interface.
    35→type internalContainerLifecycleImpl struct {
    36→	cpuManager      cpumanager.Manager
    37→	memoryManager   memorymanager.Manager
    38→	topologyManager topologymanager.Manager
    39→}
    40→
    41→func (i *internalContainerLifecycleImpl) PreStartContainer(logger klog.Logger, pod *v1.Pod, container *v1.Container, containerID string) error {
    42→	if i.cpuManager != nil {
    43→		i.cpuManager.AddContainer(logger, pod, container, containerID)
    44→	}
    45→
    46→	if i.memoryManager != nil {
    47→		i.memoryManager.AddContainer(logger, pod, container, containerID)
    48→	}
    49→
    50→	i.topologyManager.AddContainer(pod, container, containerID)
    51→
    52→	return nil
    53→}
    54→
    55→func (i *internalContainerLifecycleImpl) PostStopContainer(logger klog.Logger, containerID string) error {
    56→	return i.topologyManager.RemoveContainer(containerID)
    57→}
    58→

(\n\tv1 \"k8s.io/api/core/v1\"\n\truntimeapi \"k8s.io/cri-api/pkg/apis/runtime/v1\"\n\t\"k8s.io/klog/v2\"\n\t\"k8s.io/kubernetes/pkg/kubelet/cm/cpumanager\"\n\t\"k8s.io/kubernetes/pkg/kubelet/cm/memorymanager\"\n\t\"k8s.io/kubernetes/pkg/kubelet/cm/topologymanager\"\n)\n\ntype InternalContainerLifecycle interface {\n\tPreCreateContainer(logger klog.Logger, pod *v1.Pod, container *v1.Container, containerConfig *runtimeapi.ContainerConfig) error\n\tPreStartContainer(logger klog.Logger, pod *v1.Pod, container *v1.Container, containerID string) error\n\tPostStopContainer(logger klog.Logger, containerID string) error\n}\n\n// Implements InternalContainerLifecycle interface.\ntype internalContainerLifecycleImpl struct {\n\tcpuManager      cpumanager.Manager\n\tmemoryManager   memorymanager.Manager\n\ttopologyManager topologymanager.Manager\n}\n\nfunc (i *internalContainerLifecycleImpl) PreStartContainer(logger klog.Logger, pod *v1.Pod, container *v1.Container, containerID string) error {\n\tif i.cpuManager != nil {\n\t\ti.cpuManager.AddContainer(logger, pod, container, containerID)\n\t}\n\n\tif i.memoryManager != nil {\n\t\ti.memoryManager.AddContainer(logger, pod, container, containerID)\n\t}\n\n\ti.topologyManager.AddContainer(pod, container, containerID)\n\n\treturn nil\n}\n\nfunc (i *internalContainerLifecycleImpl) PostStopContainer(logger klog.Logger, containerID     43→// ActivePodsFunc is a function that returns a list of pods to reconcile.
    44→type ActivePodsFunc func() []*v1.Pod
    45→
    46→type runtimeService interface {
    47→	UpdateContainerResources(ctx context.Context, id string, resources *runtimeapi.ContainerResources) error
    48→}
    49→
    50→type policyName string
    51→
    52→// cpuManagerStateFileName is the file name where cpu manager stores its state
    53→const cpuManagerStateFileName = "cpu_manager_state"
    54→
    55→// Manager interface provides methods for Kubelet to manage pod cpus.
    56→type Manager interface {
    57→	// Start is called during Kubelet initialization.
    58→	// Start takes a `Context` because it may possibly spin the reconcileState helper, which in turn
    59→	// needs to update container state, which takes a context.
    60→	Start(ctx context.Context, activePods ActivePodsFunc, sourcesReady config.SourcesReady, podStatusProvider status.PodStatusProvider, containerRuntime runtimeService, initialContainers containermap.ContainerMap) error
    61→
    62→	// Called to trigger the allocation of CPUs to a container. This must be
    63→	// called at some point prior to the AddContainer() call for a container,
    64→	// e.g. at pod admission time.
    65→	Allocate(pod *v1.Pod, container *v1.Container) error
    66→
    67→	// AddContainer adds the mapping between container ID to pod UID and the container name
    68→	// The mapping used to remove the CPU allocation during the container removal
    69→	AddContainer(logger logr.Logger, p *v1.Pod, c *v1.Container, containerID string)
    70→
    71→	// RemoveContainer is called after Kubelet decides to kill or delete a
    72→	// container. After this call, the CPU manager stops trying to reconcile
    73→	// that container and any CPUs dedicated to the container are freed.
    74→	RemoveContainer(logger logr.Logger, containerID string) error
    75→
    76→	// State returns a read-only interface to the internal CPU manager state.
    77→	State() state.Reader
    78→
    79→	// GetTopologyHints implements the topologymanager.HintProvider Interface
    80→	// and is consulted to achieve NUMA aware resource alignment among this
    81→	// and other resource controllers.
    82→	GetTopologyHints(pod *v1.Pod, container *v1.Container) map[string][]topologymanager.TopologyHint
    83→
    84→	// GetExclusiveCPUs implements the podresources.CPUsProvider interface to provide
    85→	// exclusively allocated cpus for the container
    86→	GetExclusiveCPUs(podUID, containerName string) cpuset.CPUSet
    87→
    88→	// GetPodTopologyHints implements the topologymanager.HintProvider Interface
    89→	// and is consulted to achieve NUMA aware resource alignment per Pod
    90→	// among this and other resource controllers.
    91→	GetPodTopologyHints(pod *v1.Pod) map[string][]topologymanager.TopologyHint
    92→
    93→	// GetAllocatableCPUs returns the total set of CPUs available for allocation.
    94→	GetAllocatableCPUs() cpuset.CPUSet
    95→
    96→	// GetCPUAffinity returns cpuset which includes cpus from shared pools
    97→	// as well as exclusively allocated cpus
    98→	GetCPUAffinity(podUID, containerName string) cpuset.CPUSet
    99→
   100→	// GetAllCPUs returns all the CPUs known by cpumanager, as reported by the
   101→	// hardware discovery. Maps to the CPU capacity.
   102→	GetAllCPUs() cpuset.CPUSet
   103→}
   104→
   105→type manager struct {
   106→	sync.Mutex
   107→	policy Policy
   108→
   109→	// reconcilePeriod is the duration between calls to reconcileState.
   110→	reconcilePeriod time.Duration
   111→
   112→	// state allows pluggable CPU assignment policies while sharing a common
   113→	// representation of state for the system to inspect and reconcile.
   114→	state state.State
   115→
   116→	// lastUpdatedstate holds state for each container from the last time it was updated.
   117→	lastUpdateState state.State
   118→
   119→	// containerRuntime is the container runtime service interface needed
   120→	// to make UpdateContainerResources() calls against the containers.

After this call, the CPU manager stops trying to reconcile\n\t// that container and any CPUs dedicated to the container are freed.\n\tRemoveContainer(logger logr.Logger, containerID string) error\n\n\t// State returns a read-only interface to the internal CPU manager state.\n\tState() state.Reader\n\n\t// GetTopologyHints implements the topologymanager.HintProvider Interface\n\t// and is consulted to achieve NUMA aware resource alignment among this\n\t// and other resource controllers.\n\tGetTopologyHints(pod *v1.Pod, container *v1.Container) map[string][]topologymanager.TopologyHint\n\n\t// GetExclusiveCPUs implements the podresources.CPUsProvider interface to provide\n\t// exclusively allocated cpus for the container\n\tGetExclusiveCPUs(podUID, containerName string) cpuset.CPUSet\n\n\t// GetPodTopologyHints implements the topologymanager.HintProvider Interface\n\t// and is consulted to achieve NUMA aware resource alignment per Pod\n\t// among this and other resource controllers.\n\tGetPodTopologyHints(pod *v1.Pod) map[string][]topologymanager.TopologyHint\n\n\t// GetAllocatableCPUs returns the total set of CPUs available for allocation.\n\tGetAllocatableCPUs() cpuset.CPUSet\n\n\t// GetCPUAffinity returns cpuset which includes cpus from shared pools\n\t// as well as exclusively allocated cpus\n\tGetCPUAffinity(podUID, containerName string) cpuset.CPUSet\n\n\t// GetAllCPUs returns all the CPUs known by cpumanager, as reported by the\n\t// hardware discovery. Maps to the CPU capacity.\n\tGetAllCPUs() cpuset.CPUSet\n}\n\ntype manager struct {\n\tsync.Mutex\n\tpolicy Policy\n\n\t// reconcilePeriod is the duration between calls to reconcileState.\n\treconcilePeriod time.Duration\n\n\t// state allows pluggable CPU assignment policies while sharing a common\n\t// representation of state for the system to inspect and reconcile.\n\tstate state.State\n\n\t// lastUpdatedstate holds state for each container from the last time it was updated.\n\tlastUpdateState state.State\n\n\t// containerRuntime is the container runtime service interface needed\n\t// to make UpdateContainerResources() calls against the containers.", "numLines": 120, "startLine": 1, "totalLines": 546}}

    95→	Allocate(pod *v1.Pod, container *v1.Container) error
    96→}

arguments.content:
# Kubelet Container Manager: Subsystem Architecture Guide

## 1. Subsystem Purpose, Boundaries, and Dependencies

### Purpose

The kubelet container manager (`pkg/kubelet/cm`) is the subsystem responsible for translating pod resource specifications into kernel-enforced constraints on the node. Its core responsibilities are:

- Structuring the cgroup hierarchy that every pod and container lives under
- Enforcing node-level resource reservations (system, kube-reserved, node allocatable)
- Coordinating CPU, memory, and device allocation across competing pods
- Providing topology-aware placement on multi-NUMA nodes
- Bridging third-party hardware (via device plugins and DRA) into the container runtime

### Boundaries

**Upstream consumers:**
- `pkg/kubelet/kubelet.go` constructs the manager via `cm.NewContainerManager` and calls `ContainerManager.Start()`.
- `pkg/kubelet/lifecycle` calls `GetAllocateResourcesPodAdmitHandler()` to gate pod admission.
- Pod workers call `InternalContainerLifecycle().PreCreateContainer()` / `PreStartContainer()` / `PostStopContainer()` at each container lifecycle event.

**Downstream dependencies:**
- `github.com/opencontainers/cgroups` — kernel cgroup interactions (v1 and v2)
- `k8s.io/cri-api/pkg/apis` (`RuntimeService`) — container runtime calls for resource updates
- `github.com/google/cadvisor` — machine topology and capacity discovery
- `pkg/kubelet/pluginmanager` — device plugin and DRA plugin registration

### What the Manager Does Not Do

- It does not schedule or select which node a pod lands on (that is the scheduler's role).
- It does not execute containers — that is the container runtime's role.
- It does not handle network or storage resource classes; those are separate kubelet subsystems.

---

## 2. Key Components and Responsibility Split

The subsystem is composed of a central coordinator (`containerManagerImpl`) and several specialized sub-managers, each owning a distinct resource class.

### 2.1 `ContainerManager` Interface

Defined in `pkg/kubelet/cm/container_manager.go:66`.

This is the single surface that the rest of kubelet uses. Key method groups:

| Method group | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `Start(...)` | Initialize all sub-managers and cgroup hierarchy at kubelet startup |
| `GetAllocateResourcesPodAdmitHandler()` | Returns the topology manager as a `PodAdmitHandler` |
| `InternalContainerLifecycle()` | Container lifecycle hooks for allocation and cleanup |
| `GetResources(ctx, pod, container)` | Retrieve device/DRA runtime options for a container |
| `UpdatePluginResources(...)` | Gate pod admission against device plugin capacity |
| `NewPodContainerManager()` | Factory for per-pod cgroup management |
| `GetCapacity / GetDevicePluginResourceCapacity` | Report node resource totals to kubelet for scheduling |
| `Updates()` | Channel delivering device/DRA health-change events |

### 2.2 `containerManagerImpl` (Linux)

Defined in `pkg/kubelet/cm/container_manager_linux.go:100`.

The concrete Linux implementation embeds:

```go
type containerManagerImpl struct {
    cgroupManager   CgroupManager           // raw cgroup operations
    qosContainerManager QOSContainerManager // Guaranteed/Burstable/BestEffort hierarchy
    deviceManager   devicemanager.Manager
    cpuManager      cpumanager.Manager
    memoryManager   memorymanager.Manager
    topologyManager topologymanager.Manager
    draManager      *dra.Manager            // Dynamic Resource Allocation (feature-gated)
    resourceUpdates chan resourceupdates.Update
    // cadvisor, mountUtil, nodeInfo, subsystems, capacity, cgroupRoot, recorder ...
}
```

### 2.3 `CgroupManager`

Interface defined in `pkg/kubelet/cm/types.go:64`.

Abstracts over two kernel cgroup APIs:
- **cgroupfs** (`pkg/kubelet/cm/cgroup_v1_manager_linux.go`, `cgroup_v2_manager_linux.go`) — direct filesystem writes
- **systemd** (`pkg/kubelet/cm/cgroup_manager_linux.go`) — D-Bus unit management

`CgroupName` is an internal path representation (`[]string{"kubepods","burstable","podUID"}`). `CgroupName.ToSystemd()` produces the systemd slice path; `ParseCgroupfsToCgroupName()` parses the cgroupfs path. The `-` vs `_` escaping scheme in `cgroup_manager_linux.go:68` is an intentional invariant: internal names forbid `_` so the systemd round-trip is always reversible.

### 2.4 `QOSContainerManager`

Defined in `pkg/kubelet/cm/qos_container_manager_linux.go:48`.

Maintains three top-level cgroups under the `kubepods` root:
- `kubepods/guaranteed`
- `kubepods/burstable`
- `kubepods/besteffort`

Every minute (`periodicQOSCgroupUpdateInterval = 1*time.Minute`) it recomputes and applies resource limits for the burstable and best-effort tiers based on current active-pod state. This ensures that best-effort workloads are bounded to whatever headroom remains after guaranteed and burstable pods are accounted for.

### 2.5 `PodContainerManager`

Interface defined in `pkg/kubelet/cm/types.go:109`.

One instance per pod. `EnsureExists()` creates a pod-level cgroup leaf under the appropriate QoS parent. Destroyed when the pod is removed. If `CgroupsPerQOS` is false, the noop implementation (`pod_container_manager_stub.go`) is returned instead.

### 2.6 CPU Manager (`pkg/kubelet/cm/cpumanager/`)

Manages exclusive CPU pinning for Guaranteed QoS containers with integer CPU requests. Core types:

- `Manager` interface (`cpu_manager.go:56`) — lifecycle + `Allocate` + topology hints
- `Policy` interface (`policy.go`) — pluggable allocation strategies
- `state.State` interface (`state/state.go`) — persistent + in-memory allocation bookkeeping

Two policies ship:
- **none** (`policy_none.go`) — all containers share the default CPUSet; no pinning
- **static** (`policy_static.go`) — exclusively pins CPUs for eligible containers; maintains shared, reserved, and exclusive CPU pools

### 2.7 Memory Manager (`pkg/kubelet/cm/memorymanager/`)

Manages NUMA-pinned memory allocation for Guaranteed QoS workloads, including hugepages. Follows the same three-layer design as the CPU manager: `Manager` → `Policy` → `State`. The static policy allocates memory blocks from specific NUMA nodes.

### 2.8 Topology Manager (`pkg/kubelet/cm/topologymanager/`)

The cross-cutting coordinator. Defined in `topology_manager.go:58`, it implements `lifecycle.PodAdmitHandler` and is returned directly by `GetAllocateResourcesPodAdmitHandler()` (`container_manager_linux.go:760`). All other resource managers register as `HintProvider` instances with the topology manager.

### 2.9 Device Manager (`pkg/kubelet/cm/devicemanager/`)

Manages device plugin resources (GPUs, FPGAs, NICs, etc.) via the v1beta1 plugin API. Exposes topology hints when plugins report NUMA affinity and serves as the allocation backend for `GetResources`.

### 2.10 DRA Manager (`pkg/kubelet/cm/dra/`)

Feature-gated (`DynamicResourceAllocation`). Implements the structured parameter model for third-party resource claims, complementing the device plugin model with richer API semantics.

### 2.11 `InternalContainerLifecycle`

Interface defined in `pkg/kubelet/cm/internal_container_lifecycle.go:28`. Implemented by `internalContainerLifecycleImpl` which holds references to the CPU, memory, and topology managers. The container runtime bridge calls these three hooks at the container lifecycle boundaries:

```go
PreCreateContainer(pod, container, containerConfig)  // modify CRI config (e.g. cpuset annotations)
PreStartContainer(pod, container, containerID)        // register container ID → allocation
PostStopContainer(containerID)                        // release allocation
```

---

## 3. End-to-End Control Flow

### 3.1 Initialization (`ContainerManager.Start`)

`container_manager_linux.go:615`

```
kubelet calls cm.Start(ctx, node, activePods, getNode, sourcesReady, podStatusProvider, runtimeService, ...)
│
├─ buildContainerMapAndRunningSetFromRuntime()     // snapshot existing containers from CRI
├─ cm.draManager.Start(...)                         // if DRA feature gate enabled
├─ cm.cpuManager.Start(...)                         // restore checkpoint, start reconcile loop
├─ cm.memoryManager.Start(...)                      // restore checkpoint
├─ validateNodeAllocatable()
├─ setupNode(ctx, activePods)
│   ├─ validateSystemRequirements()               // check cgroup subsystem mounts
│   ├─ setupKernelTunables()                      // vm.overcommit_memory, etc.
│   ├─ createNodeAllocatableCgroups()             // mkdir kubepods/
│   ├─ qosContainerManager.Start()               // create Guaranteed/Burstable/BestEffort cgroups
│   ├─ enforceNodeAllocatableCgroups()            // set kubepods/ limits; retries until eviction frees memory
│   └─ configure systemContainers (runtime, kubelet, system cgroups)
│
├─ start periodic system container ensure-state goroutine (every 1 min)
├─ start periodic task goroutine (every 5 min)
└─ cm.deviceManager.Start(...)                    // connect to device plugin sockets, reconcile
```

**Manager construction order matters** (`container_manager_linux.go:296`):
1. Topology manager created first (it holds no references to other managers)
2. Device manager created, then registered as `HintProvider`
3. CPU manager created, then registered as `HintProvider`
4. Memory manager created, then registered as `HintProvider`

This ordering ensures the topology manager's `Store` interface is available when sub-managers need to query the merged affinity hint.

### 3.2 Pod Admission Flow

```
kubelet admission loop
│
└─ lifecycle.PodAdmitHandler.Admit(attrs)
    │  (handler is cm.topologyManager, returned by GetAllocateResourcesPodAdmitHandler())
    │
    └─ topologymanager.Admit(attrs)          // topology_manager.go
        │
        ├─ For each container (container scope) OR for the pod (pod scope):
        │   ├─ Collect hints: cpuManager.GetTopologyHints(pod, container)
        │   ├─ Collect hints: memoryManager.GetTopologyHints(pod, container)
        │   ├─ Collect hints: deviceManager.GetTopologyHints(pod, container)
        │   └─ Merge hints per policy → best TopologyHint stored in scope.podTopologyHints
        │
        └─ For each container:
            ├─ Call cpuManager.Allocate(pod, container)
            │   └─ policy.Allocate(s, pod, container)
            │       └─ (static policy) query topologyManager.GetAffinity() for NUMA mask
            │          → allocateCPUs() using cpuAccumulator (cpu_assignment.go)
            │          → persist to state (checkpoint)
            ├─ Call memoryManager.Allocate(pod, container)
            │   └─ policy.Allocate(s, pod, container)
            │       └─ query topologyManager.GetAffinity() for NUMA mask
            │          → allocate memory blocks from matching NUMA nodes
            │          → persist to state (checkpoint)
            └─ Call deviceManager.Allocate(pod, container)
                └─ contact device plugin via gRPC Allocate() RPC
                   → store DeviceRunContainerOptions in checkpoint
```

**Key invariant:** The topology manager calls `GetTopologyHints` to *compute* the merged NUMA affinity and stores it. It then calls `Allocate` on each hint provider, which *reads back* the stored affinity via `Store.GetAffinity(podUID, containerName)`. The two-phase design prevents circular dependencies between managers.

If no feasible intersection of NUMA hints exists under the chosen policy, the topology manager returns a `TopologyAffinityError`, and the pod is rejected at admission with a descriptive event.

### 3.3 Container Start Flow

```
Pod worker → container runtime manager
│
└─ InternalContainerLifecycle().PreCreateContainer(pod, container, containerConfig)
    └─ (Linux: internal_container_lifecycle_linux.go)
       cpuset annotation or CRI cpu-set fields populated from cpu manager state
│
└─ CRI CreateContainer()
│
└─ InternalContainerLifecycle().PreStartContainer(pod, container, containerID)
    ├─ cpuManager.AddContainer(pod, container, containerID)   // register ID→alloc mapping
    ├─ memoryManager.AddContainer(pod, container, containerID) // same
    └─ topologyManager.AddContainer(pod, container, containerID) // track for cleanup
│
└─ CRI StartContainer()
```

### 3.4 CPU Reconciliation Loop

Started inside `cpuManager.Start()`. Runs every `CPUManagerReconcilePeriod` (default 10 s, configurable via `--cpu-manager-reconcile-period`):

```
reconcileState()
│
├─ for each active pod/container:
│   ├─ look up assigned CPUSet in state
│   ├─ look up last-applied CPUSet in lastUpdateState
│   └─ if different: runtimeService.UpdateContainerResources(containerID, cpuSet)
│
└─ for stale containers (in state but not in active pods, once sourcesReady):
    └─ policy.RemoveContainer(state, podUID, containerName)
```

This loop is the safety net: even if the `PreCreateContainer` path missed a container (e.g., kubelet restart), the reconcile loop re-establishes the correct CPU affinity.

### 3.5 Container Stop Flow

```
Pod worker terminates container
│
└─ InternalContainerLifecycle().PostStopContainer(containerID)
    └─ topologyManager.RemoveContainer(containerID)
        └─ scope.RemoveContainer(containerID)  // remove from podTopologyHints map
```

CPU and memory allocations are cleaned up lazily in the reconcile loop when the container is no longer in the active pods set.

### 3.6 Resource Update Channel

`container_manager_linux.go:360–389` sets up a fan-in goroutine that merges update channels from device manager and DRA manager into a single `chan resourceupdates.Update`. The kubelet main sync loop consumes `cm.Updates()` to trigger pod status re-evaluation when device health changes.

---

## 4. Failure Modes, Limits, and Design Tradeoffs

### 4.1 Topology Manager NUMA Node Limit

`topology_manager.go:42`:
```go
defaultMaxAllowableNUMANodes = 8
```
The hint-merging algorithm enumerates all permutations of hints across providers. With *N* NUMA nodes, each provider can generate up to 2^N − 1 possible affinity masks. With 8 NUMA nodes and 3 providers, the state space is manageable; beyond 8 nodes, initialization fails with an error rather than produce unacceptably slow admission checks. This is a hard architectural limit.

### 4.2 CPU Manager Static Policy Constraints

- **Integer CPUs only**: Fractional CPU requests never receive exclusive pinning. A container requesting `0.5` CPU shares the pool.
- **Reserved CPUs must be non-zero**: The shared pool is defined as `allCPUs − reserved − exclusive`. If no CPUs are reserved and all guaranteed pods claim all CPUs, best-effort pods may starve. The policy validation prevents `ReservedSystemCPUs` from being empty on nodes with Guaranteed pods.
- **SMT alignment**: The `FullPhysicalCPUsOnly` option prevents allocating threads from the same physical core to different containers, eliminating noisy-neighbor cache interference at the cost of lower packing density.

### 4.3 Checkpoint Persistence vs. In-Memory State

CPU, memory, and device managers all use a checkpoint file under `KubeletRootDir` (e.g., `/var/lib/kubelet/cpu_manager_state`). On kubelet restart:
- Allocations from prior containers are restored.
- If the checkpoint format changes across versions, the manager performs a policy-name comparison and resets state if inconsistent.
- Loss of the checkpoint directory (e.g., ephemeral-root node) means all allocations are forgotten; the reconcile loop re-establishes CPU affinity for running containers but cannot recover device allocations for deleted pods.

### 4.4 Node Allocatable Enforcement Race

`node_container_manager_linux.go:95` shows that if the node has running pods whose memory usage exceeds the new `kubepods` cgroup limit (e.g., after lowering `--kube-reserved`), the `cgroupManager.Update()` call will fail. The code retries in a goroutine indefinitely until eviction frees memory. This retry loop does not have a deadline, so the node may emit repeated warning events for an extended period during eviction. Memory limit enforcement is therefore best-effort with eventual consistency.

### 4.5 cgroup v1 Deprecation

`container_manager.go:52`:
```go
CgroupV1DeprecatedWarning = "cgroup v1 detected. cgroup v1 support is deprecated and will be removed in a future release."
```
The system validates required subsystem mounts at startup (`validateSystemRequirements`). Under cgroup v2 unified hierarchy, cpu quota hard-capping is unconditionally available. Under cgroup v1, cpu quota requires both `cpu.cfs_period_us` and `cpu.cfs_quota_us` to be present; their absence degrades to soft CPU limits only (`cm.status.SoftRequirements`).

### 4.6 Device Plugin Health Propagation

When a device transitions from healthy to unhealthy, the device manager sends a `resourceupdates.Update` on its channel. The kubelet sync loop re-evaluates affected pods. However, previously allocated unhealthy devices are not automatically revoked from running containers — the device plugin is responsible for handling its own device failure semantics. This is a deliberate interface boundary: the device manager tracks status but does not forcibly kill containers when hardware fails.

### 4.7 DRA vs. Device Plugin Coexistence

Both DRA (`draManager`) and classic device plugins (`deviceManager`) can be active simultaneously (controlled by the `DynamicResourceAllocation` feature gate). They use different plugin registration handlers (`DRAPlugin` vs. `DevicePlugin` in `GetPluginRegistrationHandlers`). A resource requested via DRA claims is handled entirely by `draManager.GetResources()` and never passes through `deviceManager`. The two paths do not interfere but cannot share a device instance.

---

## 5. Extension Points, Customization Hooks, and Associated Risks

### 5.1 CPU Manager Policies via Options

The static CPU policy accepts a `CPUManagerPolicyOptions` map passed at kubelet startup. Options such as `full-pcpus-only`, `distribute-cpus-across-numa`, and `prefer-align-cpus-by-uncore-cache` alter the `cpuAccumulator` allocation strategy in `cpu_assignment.go` without requiring a different policy name. Adding a new option only requires implementing a new code path inside `policy_static.go:Allocate` and declaring the option in the feature-gated options registry.

**Risk**: Options interact. `distribute-cpus-across-numa` and `full-pcpus-only` together may produce no valid allocation on nodes with odd CPU counts, causing guaranteed pods to be perpetually rejected. There is no static validation of option combinations; failures surface only at runtime as admission errors.

### 5.2 Topology Manager Policies

Four policy implementations exist in `pkg/kubelet/cm/topologymanager/`: `none`, `best-effort`, `restricted`, `single-numa-node`. They share the `HintMerger` in `policy.go` but differ in how they handle non-preferred hints. New policies can be added by implementing the `Policy` interface (`Name() string; Merge(logger, providersHints) (TopologyHint, bool)`) and registering in `NewManager`.

**Risk**: A custom policy that always returns `preferred=true` for any affinity mask will never reject a pod, silently undermining NUMA locality guarantees without surfacing errors.

### 5.3 Topology Manager Scopes

Two scopes exist (`scope_container.go`, `scope_pod.go`). Pod scope aggregates hints across all containers before merging; container scope merges per-container independently. Switching from container to pod scope on a running node with allocated Guaranteed pods requires draining the node, because pod scope may accept pods that container scope would reject (or vice versa).

### 5.4 Device Plugins

Third-party device plugins implement the v1beta1 gRPC API in `pkg/kubelet/cm/devicemanager/plugin/v1beta1/`. Plugins must implement `ListAndWatch`, `Allocate`, and optionally `GetTopologyHints`. The device manager calls `GetTopologyHints` during the topology manager's admission phase; a plugin that returns inaccurate NUMA topology hints will cause the topology manager to make suboptimal placements without any error signal.

Plugins that return non-reproducible allocations across `Allocate` calls break the checkpoint restore invariant: after kubelet restart, the checkpoint may contain a device ID that the plugin no longer knows about.

### 5.5 `HintProvider` Registration

`cm.topologyManager.AddHintProvider(logger, provider)` in `container_manager_linux.go:312,341,356` is called during construction. This is not a runtime extension point; there is no way to register a new hint provider after `NewContainerManager` returns. In-tree managers are the only registered providers today.

### 5.6 `InternalContainerLifecycle` Hooks

`PreCreateContainer` in `internal_container_lifecycle_linux.go` is the place where CRI container config is modified (e.g., to inject CPU affinity annotations). Adding a new resource type that needs CRI-level config injection requires extending this hook. The interface is internal (`package cm`) and not a stable extension point for external components.

---

## 6. Operational Implications

### 6.1 Performance

**Admission latency**: Every pod admission call traverses the topology manager, which in turn calls `GetTopologyHints` on all registered providers synchronously. For a cluster with 3 resource managers and up to 8 NUMA nodes, the worst-case hint permutation count is O((2^8)^3) ≈ 16M. In practice, constraints reduce this by orders of magnitude, but nodes with many NUMA nodes and many resource types will exhibit measurable admission latency. Monitor `kubelet_topology_manager_admission_duration_ms` (emitted via `metrics`) to detect regressions.

**CPU reconcile loop overhead**: The reconcile loop calls `runtimeService.UpdateContainerResources()` for every container whose CPU assignment differs from the last recorded state. On a node with hundreds of containers, the loop may generate significant CRI traffic every `CPUManagerReconcilePeriod`. Widen the period (`--cpu-manager-reconcile-period`) to reduce CRI overhead if CPU affinity changes are infrequent in your workload.

**QoS cgroup update interval**: `qosContainerManager` recomputes burstable and best-effort cgroup limits every minute. On a busy node with rapidly changing pod counts, this update may arrive 60 s after a pod terminates, leaving residual memory headroom unclaimed. This is acceptable for typical workloads but can cause transient OOM pressure immediately after high-density pod churn.

### 6.2 Reliability

**Kubelet restart recovery**: CPU and memory managers restore state from checkpoint before `sourcesReady` is declared. Until sources are ready, stale state removal is suppressed (`reconcileState` skips removal). This window ensures that containers started by a prior kubelet invocation are not deprived of their CPU affinity during the brief period when pod sources are still being synchronized.

**Device plugin disconnection**: The device manager uses gRPC streaming (`ListAndWatch`) to receive device health updates. If the plugin crashes or disconnects, its devices are immediately marked unhealthy in the manager's internal map. Capacity reported via `GetDevicePluginResourceCapacity` drops to zero for that resource, preventing new pods from being scheduled to the node. Running pods retain their device files — the kernel does not revoke them — but the plugin is no longer available to handle `Allocate` for new containers.

**cgroup write failures**: `enforceNodeAllocatableCgroups` retries memory limit writes indefinitely when running pods' memory exceeds the target limit. This is the intended behavior: forcibly setting a lower limit would immediately OOM-kill running workloads. However, if eviction is misconfigured or stalled, the node will emit repeated `FailedNodeAllocatableEnforcement` events without ever converging. Operators should monitor for this event as a signal that eviction thresholds need tuning.

### 6.3 Maintainability

**Platform divergence**: `containerManagerImpl` has separate concrete implementations under build tags `linux`, `windows`, and the stub (`container_manager_stub.go`). The Windows implementation (`container_manager_windows.go`) contains a minimal struct with `deviceManager`, `topologyManager`, `cpuManager`, and `memoryManager` fields, but cgroup enforcement and QoS hierarchy are absent. Any change to `ContainerManager` interface in `container_manager.go` must be reflected in all three platform implementations. The mock in `pkg/kubelet/cm/testing/mocks.go` is auto-generated from the interface and must be regenerated after interface changes (`//go:generate mockery` in `container_manager.go:17`).

**State schema evolution**: Checkpoint files embed a policy name. If the policy name changes (e.g., a rename of `"static"` to `"pinned"`), kubelet will consider the checkpoint invalid and reset all allocations at startup. This means all Guaranteed pods must be restarted to regain exclusive CPU/memory pinning after such a policy rename.

---

## 7. File and Directory Map

| Path | Role |
|---|---|
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/container_manager.go` | `ContainerManager` interface definition; `NodeConfig`, `NodeAllocatableConfig`, `Status` types |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/container_manager_linux.go` | Linux `containerManagerImpl`: construction, `Start()`, wiring of sub-managers |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/container_manager_windows.go` | Windows stub: minimal `containerManagerImpl`, no cgroup hierarchy |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/container_manager_stub.go` | Unsupported-platform stub |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/types.go` | `CgroupManager`, `PodContainerManager`, `CgroupName`, `ResourceConfig`, `CgroupConfig` interfaces and types |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/internal_container_lifecycle.go` | `InternalContainerLifecycle` interface; `internalContainerLifecycleImpl` implementation |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/internal_container_lifecycle_linux.go` | Linux-specific `PreCreateContainer` (CRI config injection) |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/cgroup_manager_linux.go` | `CgroupName.ToSystemd()`, name escaping, `NewCgroupManager` dispatch |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/cgroup_v1_manager_linux.go` | cgroupfs v1 CRUD operations |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/cgroup_v2_manager_linux.go` | cgroupfs v2 unified hierarchy CRUD operations |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/qos_container_manager_linux.go` | `QOSContainerManager`: Guaranteed/Burstable/BestEffort cgroup management |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/node_container_manager_linux.go` | Node allocatable cgroup creation and enforcement with retry logic |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/pod_container_manager_linux.go` | `PodContainerManager`: per-pod cgroup lifecycle |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/cpumanager/cpu_manager.go` | CPU `Manager` interface; `manager` struct; reconcile loop |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/cpumanager/policy_static.go` | Static CPU policy: `Allocate`, `allocateCPUs`, NUMA-aware assignment |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/cpumanager/cpu_assignment.go` | `cpuAccumulator`: topology-aware CPU selection algorithm |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/cpumanager/state/state_checkpoint.go` | Checkpoint-backed state persistence |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/memorymanager/memory_manager.go` | Memory `Manager` interface and implementation |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/memorymanager/policy_static.go` | Static memory policy: NUMA-pinned allocation |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/topologymanager/topology_manager.go` | `Manager` and `HintProvider` interfaces; `TopologyAffinityError`; NUMA node limit |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/topologymanager/policy.go` | `HintMerger`; hint permutation and scoring logic |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/topologymanager/scope_container.go` | Container-scope admission logic |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/topologymanager/scope_pod.go` | Pod-scope admission logic |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/topologymanager/bitmask/bitmask.go` | NUMA affinity bitmask operations |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/devicemanager/manager.go` | Device plugin `Manager` implementation; health tracking; allocation |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/devicemanager/pod_devices.go` | Per-pod device allocation tracking and checkpoint |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/devicemanager/topology_hints.go` | Device topology hint generation |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/devicemanager/plugin/v1beta1/` | Device plugin gRPC server/client and registration |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/dra/` | Dynamic Resource Allocation manager (feature-gated) |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/resourceupdates/` | `Update` type for device/DRA health change events |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/admission/errors.go` | Admission error helpers |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/testing/mocks.go` | Auto-generated mock for `ContainerManager` (used in unit tests) |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/containermap/` | `ContainerMap` type: maps container IDs to pod UID + name during restart recovery |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/util/` | Helpers: cgroup path utilities |

input.content:
# Kubelet Container Manager: Subsystem Architecture Guide

## 1. Subsystem Purpose, Boundaries, and Dependencies

### Purpose

The kubelet container manager (`pkg/kubelet/cm`) is the subsystem responsible for translating pod resource specifications into kernel-enforced constraints on the node. Its core responsibilities are:

- Structuring the cgroup hierarchy that every pod and container lives under
- Enforcing node-level resource reservations (system, kube-reserved, node allocatable)
- Coordinating CPU, memory, and device allocation across competing pods
- Providing topology-aware placement on multi-NUMA nodes
- Bridging third-party hardware (via device plugins and DRA) into the container runtime

### Boundaries

**Upstream consumers:**
- `pkg/kubelet/kubelet.go` constructs the manager via `cm.NewContainerManager` and calls `ContainerManager.Start()`.
- `pkg/kubelet/lifecycle` calls `GetAllocateResourcesPodAdmitHandler()` to gate pod admission.
- Pod workers call `InternalContainerLifecycle().PreCreateContainer()` / `PreStartContainer()` / `PostStopContainer()` at each container lifecycle event.

**Downstream dependencies:**
- `github.com/opencontainers/cgroups` — kernel cgroup interactions (v1 and v2)
- `k8s.io/cri-api/pkg/apis` (`RuntimeService`) — container runtime calls for resource updates
- `github.com/google/cadvisor` — machine topology and capacity discovery
- `pkg/kubelet/pluginmanager` — device plugin and DRA plugin registration

### What the Manager Does Not Do

- It does not schedule or select which node a pod lands on (that is the scheduler's role).
- It does not execute containers — that is the container runtime's role.
- It does not handle network or storage resource classes; those are separate kubelet subsystems.

---

## 2. Key Components and Responsibility Split

The subsystem is composed of a central coordinator (`containerManagerImpl`) and several specialized sub-managers, each owning a distinct resource class.

### 2.1 `ContainerManager` Interface

Defined in `pkg/kubelet/cm/container_manager.go:66`.

This is the single surface that the rest of kubelet uses. Key method groups:

| Method group | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `Start(...)` | Initialize all sub-managers and cgroup hierarchy at kubelet startup |
| `GetAllocateResourcesPodAdmitHandler()` | Returns the topology manager as a `PodAdmitHandler` |
| `InternalContainerLifecycle()` | Container lifecycle hooks for allocation and cleanup |
| `GetResources(ctx, pod, container)` | Retrieve device/DRA runtime options for a container |
| `UpdatePluginResources(...)` | Gate pod admission against device plugin capacity |
| `NewPodContainerManager()` | Factory for per-pod cgroup management |
| `GetCapacity / GetDevicePluginResourceCapacity` | Report node resource totals to kubelet for scheduling |
| `Updates()` | Channel delivering device/DRA health-change events |

### 2.2 `containerManagerImpl` (Linux)

Defined in `pkg/kubelet/cm/container_manager_linux.go:100`.

The concrete Linux implementation embeds:

```go
type containerManagerImpl struct {
    cgroupManager   CgroupManager           // raw cgroup operations
    qosContainerManager QOSContainerManager // Guaranteed/Burstable/BestEffort hierarchy
    deviceManager   devicemanager.Manager
    cpuManager      cpumanager.Manager
    memoryManager   memorymanager.Manager
    topologyManager topologymanager.Manager
    draManager      *dra.Manager            // Dynamic Resource Allocation (feature-gated)
    resourceUpdates chan resourceupdates.Update
    // cadvisor, mountUtil, nodeInfo, subsystems, capacity, cgroupRoot, recorder ...
}
```

### 2.3 `CgroupManager`

Interface defined in `pkg/kubelet/cm/types.go:64`.

Abstracts over two kernel cgroup APIs:
- **cgroupfs** (`pkg/kubelet/cm/cgroup_v1_manager_linux.go`, `cgroup_v2_manager_linux.go`) — direct filesystem writes
- **systemd** (`pkg/kubelet/cm/cgroup_manager_linux.go`) — D-Bus unit management

`CgroupName` is an internal path representation (`[]string{"kubepods","burstable","podUID"}`). `CgroupName.ToSystemd()` produces the systemd slice path; `ParseCgroupfsToCgroupName()` parses the cgroupfs path. The `-` vs `_` escaping scheme in `cgroup_manager_linux.go:68` is an intentional invariant: internal names forbid `_` so the systemd round-trip is always reversible.

### 2.4 `QOSContainerManager`

Defined in `pkg/kubelet/cm/qos_container_manager_linux.go:48`.

Maintains three top-level cgroups under the `kubepods` root:
- `kubepods/guaranteed`
- `kubepods/burstable`
- `kubepods/besteffort`

Every minute (`periodicQOSCgroupUpdateInterval = 1*time.Minute`) it recomputes and applies resource limits for the burstable and best-effort tiers based on current active-pod state. This ensures that best-effort workloads are bounded to whatever headroom remains after guaranteed and burstable pods are accounted for.

### 2.5 `PodContainerManager`

Interface defined in `pkg/kubelet/cm/types.go:109`.

One instance per pod. `EnsureExists()` creates a pod-level cgroup leaf under the appropriate QoS parent. Destroyed when the pod is removed. If `CgroupsPerQOS` is false, the noop implementation (`pod_container_manager_stub.go`) is returned instead.

### 2.6 CPU Manager (`pkg/kubelet/cm/cpumanager/`)

Manages exclusive CPU pinning for Guaranteed QoS containers with integer CPU requests. Core types:

- `Manager` interface (`cpu_manager.go:56`) — lifecycle + `Allocate` + topology hints
- `Policy` interface (`policy.go`) — pluggable allocation strategies
- `state.State` interface (`state/state.go`) — persistent + in-memory allocation bookkeeping

Two policies ship:
- **none** (`policy_none.go`) — all containers share the default CPUSet; no pinning
- **static** (`policy_static.go`) — exclusively pins CPUs for eligible containers; maintains shared, reserved, and exclusive CPU pools

### 2.7 Memory Manager (`pkg/kubelet/cm/memorymanager/`)

Manages NUMA-pinned memory allocation for Guaranteed QoS workloads, including hugepages. Follows the same three-layer design as the CPU manager: `Manager` → `Policy` → `State`. The static policy allocates memory blocks from specific NUMA nodes.

### 2.8 Topology Manager (`pkg/kubelet/cm/topologymanager/`)

The cross-cutting coordinator. Defined in `topology_manager.go:58`, it implements `lifecycle.PodAdmitHandler` and is returned directly by `GetAllocateResourcesPodAdmitHandler()` (`container_manager_linux.go:760`). All other resource managers register as `HintProvider` instances with the topology manager.

### 2.9 Device Manager (`pkg/kubelet/cm/devicemanager/`)

Manages device plugin resources (GPUs, FPGAs, NICs, etc.) via the v1beta1 plugin API. Exposes topology hints when plugins report NUMA affinity and serves as the allocation backend for `GetResources`.

### 2.10 DRA Manager (`pkg/kubelet/cm/dra/`)

Feature-gated (`DynamicResourceAllocation`). Implements the structured parameter model for third-party resource claims, complementing the device plugin model with richer API semantics.

### 2.11 `InternalContainerLifecycle`

Interface defined in `pkg/kubelet/cm/internal_container_lifecycle.go:28`. Implemented by `internalContainerLifecycleImpl` which holds references to the CPU, memory, and topology managers. The container runtime bridge calls these three hooks at the container lifecycle boundaries:

```go
PreCreateContainer(pod, container, containerConfig)  // modify CRI config (e.g. cpuset annotations)
PreStartContainer(pod, container, containerID)        // register container ID → allocation
PostStopContainer(containerID)                        // release allocation
```

---

## 3. End-to-End Control Flow

### 3.1 Initialization (`ContainerManager.Start`)

`container_manager_linux.go:615`

```
kubelet calls cm.Start(ctx, node, activePods, getNode, sourcesReady, podStatusProvider, runtimeService, ...)
│
├─ buildContainerMapAndRunningSetFromRuntime()     // snapshot existing containers from CRI
├─ cm.draManager.Start(...)                         // if DRA feature gate enabled
├─ cm.cpuManager.Start(...)                         // restore checkpoint, start reconcile loop
├─ cm.memoryManager.Start(...)                      // restore checkpoint
├─ validateNodeAllocatable()
├─ setupNode(ctx, activePods)
│   ├─ validateSystemRequirements()               // check cgroup subsystem mounts
│   ├─ setupKernelTunables()                      // vm.overcommit_memory, etc.
│   ├─ createNodeAllocatableCgroups()             // mkdir kubepods/
│   ├─ qosContainerManager.Start()               // create Guaranteed/Burstable/BestEffort cgroups
│   ├─ enforceNodeAllocatableCgroups()            // set kubepods/ limits; retries until eviction frees memory
│   └─ configure systemContainers (runtime, kubelet, system cgroups)
│
├─ start periodic system container ensure-state goroutine (every 1 min)
├─ start periodic task goroutine (every 5 min)
└─ cm.deviceManager.Start(...)                    // connect to device plugin sockets, reconcile
```

**Manager construction order matters** (`container_manager_linux.go:296`):
1. Topology manager created first (it holds no references to other managers)
2. Device manager created, then registered as `HintProvider`
3. CPU manager created, then registered as `HintProvider`
4. Memory manager created, then registered as `HintProvider`

This ordering ensures the topology manager's `Store` interface is available when sub-managers need to query the merged affinity hint.

### 3.2 Pod Admission Flow

```
kubelet admission loop
│
└─ lifecycle.PodAdmitHandler.Admit(attrs)
    │  (handler is cm.topologyManager, returned by GetAllocateResourcesPodAdmitHandler())
    │
    └─ topologymanager.Admit(attrs)          // topology_manager.go
        │
        ├─ For each container (container scope) OR for the pod (pod scope):
        │   ├─ Collect hints: cpuManager.GetTopologyHints(pod, container)
        │   ├─ Collect hints: memoryManager.GetTopologyHints(pod, container)
        │   ├─ Collect hints: deviceManager.GetTopologyHints(pod, container)
        │   └─ Merge hints per policy → best TopologyHint stored in scope.podTopologyHints
        │
        └─ For each container:
            ├─ Call cpuManager.Allocate(pod, container)
            │   └─ policy.Allocate(s, pod, container)
            │       └─ (static policy) query topologyManager.GetAffinity() for NUMA mask
            │          → allocateCPUs() using cpuAccumulator (cpu_assignment.go)
            │          → persist to state (checkpoint)
            ├─ Call memoryManager.Allocate(pod, container)
            │   └─ policy.Allocate(s, pod, container)
            │       └─ query topologyManager.GetAffinity() for NUMA mask
            │          → allocate memory blocks from matching NUMA nodes
            │          → persist to state (checkpoint)
            └─ Call deviceManager.Allocate(pod, container)
                └─ contact device plugin via gRPC Allocate() RPC
                   → store DeviceRunContainerOptions in checkpoint
```

**Key invariant:** The topology manager calls `GetTopologyHints` to *compute* the merged NUMA affinity and stores it. It then calls `Allocate` on each hint provider, which *reads back* the stored affinity via `Store.GetAffinity(podUID, containerName)`. The two-phase design prevents circular dependencies between managers.

If no feasible intersection of NUMA hints exists under the chosen policy, the topology manager returns a `TopologyAffinityError`, and the pod is rejected at admission with a descriptive event.

### 3.3 Container Start Flow

```
Pod worker → container runtime manager
│
└─ InternalContainerLifecycle().PreCreateContainer(pod, container, containerConfig)
    └─ (Linux: internal_container_lifecycle_linux.go)
       cpuset annotation or CRI cpu-set fields populated from cpu manager state
│
└─ CRI CreateContainer()
│
└─ InternalContainerLifecycle().PreStartContainer(pod, container, containerID)
    ├─ cpuManager.AddContainer(pod, container, containerID)   // register ID→alloc mapping
    ├─ memoryManager.AddContainer(pod, container, containerID) // same
    └─ topologyManager.AddContainer(pod, container, containerID) // track for cleanup
│
└─ CRI StartContainer()
```

### 3.4 CPU Reconciliation Loop

Started inside `cpuManager.Start()`. Runs every `CPUManagerReconcilePeriod` (default 10 s, configurable via `--cpu-manager-reconcile-period`):

```
reconcileState()
│
├─ for each active pod/container:
│   ├─ look up assigned CPUSet in state
│   ├─ look up last-applied CPUSet in lastUpdateState
│   └─ if different: runtimeService.UpdateContainerResources(containerID, cpuSet)
│
└─ for stale containers (in state but not in active pods, once sourcesReady):
    └─ policy.RemoveContainer(state, podUID, containerName)
```

This loop is the safety net: even if the `PreCreateContainer` path missed a container (e.g., kubelet restart), the reconcile loop re-establishes the correct CPU affinity.

### 3.5 Container Stop Flow

```
Pod worker terminates container
│
└─ InternalContainerLifecycle().PostStopContainer(containerID)
    └─ topologyManager.RemoveContainer(containerID)
        └─ scope.RemoveContainer(containerID)  // remove from podTopologyHints map
```

CPU and memory allocations are cleaned up lazily in the reconcile loop when the container is no longer in the active pods set.

### 3.6 Resource Update Channel

`container_manager_linux.go:360–389` sets up a fan-in goroutine that merges update channels from device manager and DRA manager into a single `chan resourceupdates.Update`. The kubelet main sync loop consumes `cm.Updates()` to trigger pod status re-evaluation when device health changes.

---

## 4. Failure Modes, Limits, and Design Tradeoffs

### 4.1 Topology Manager NUMA Node Limit

`topology_manager.go:42`:
```go
defaultMaxAllowableNUMANodes = 8
```
The hint-merging algorithm enumerates all permutations of hints across providers. With *N* NUMA nodes, each provider can generate up to 2^N − 1 possible affinity masks. With 8 NUMA nodes and 3 providers, the state space is manageable; beyond 8 nodes, initialization fails with an error rather than produce unacceptably slow admission checks. This is a hard architectural limit.

### 4.2 CPU Manager Static Policy Constraints

- **Integer CPUs only**: Fractional CPU requests never receive exclusive pinning. A container requesting `0.5` CPU shares the pool.
- **Reserved CPUs must be non-zero**: The shared pool is defined as `allCPUs − reserved − exclusive`. If no CPUs are reserved and all guaranteed pods claim all CPUs, best-effort pods may starve. The policy validation prevents `ReservedSystemCPUs` from being empty on nodes with Guaranteed pods.
- **SMT alignment**: The `FullPhysicalCPUsOnly` option prevents allocating threads from the same physical core to different containers, eliminating noisy-neighbor cache interference at the cost of lower packing density.

### 4.3 Checkpoint Persistence vs. In-Memory State

CPU, memory, and device managers all use a checkpoint file under `KubeletRootDir` (e.g., `/var/lib/kubelet/cpu_manager_state`). On kubelet restart:
- Allocations from prior containers are restored.
- If the checkpoint format changes across versions, the manager performs a policy-name comparison and resets state if inconsistent.
- Loss of the checkpoint directory (e.g., ephemeral-root node) means all allocations are forgotten; the reconcile loop re-establishes CPU affinity for running containers but cannot recover device allocations for deleted pods.

### 4.4 Node Allocatable Enforcement Race

`node_container_manager_linux.go:95` shows that if the node has running pods whose memory usage exceeds the new `kubepods` cgroup limit (e.g., after lowering `--kube-reserved`), the `cgroupManager.Update()` call will fail. The code retries in a goroutine indefinitely until eviction frees memory. This retry loop does not have a deadline, so the node may emit repeated warning events for an extended period during eviction. Memory limit enforcement is therefore best-effort with eventual consistency.

### 4.5 cgroup v1 Deprecation

`container_manager.go:52`:
```go
CgroupV1DeprecatedWarning = "cgroup v1 detected. cgroup v1 support is deprecated and will be removed in a future release."
```
The system validates required subsystem mounts at startup (`validateSystemRequirements`). Under cgroup v2 unified hierarchy, cpu quota hard-capping is unconditionally available. Under cgroup v1, cpu quota requires both `cpu.cfs_period_us` and `cpu.cfs_quota_us` to be present; their absence degrades to soft CPU limits only (`cm.status.SoftRequirements`).

### 4.6 Device Plugin Health Propagation

When a device transitions from healthy to unhealthy, the device manager sends a `resourceupdates.Update` on its channel. The kubelet sync loop re-evaluates affected pods. However, previously allocated unhealthy devices are not automatically revoked from running containers — the device plugin is responsible for handling its own device failure semantics. This is a deliberate interface boundary: the device manager tracks status but does not forcibly kill containers when hardware fails.

### 4.7 DRA vs. Device Plugin Coexistence

Both DRA (`draManager`) and classic device plugins (`deviceManager`) can be active simultaneously (controlled by the `DynamicResourceAllocation` feature gate). They use different plugin registration handlers (`DRAPlugin` vs. `DevicePlugin` in `GetPluginRegistrationHandlers`). A resource requested via DRA claims is handled entirely by `draManager.GetResources()` and never passes through `deviceManager`. The two paths do not interfere but cannot share a device instance.

---

## 5. Extension Points, Customization Hooks, and Associated Risks

### 5.1 CPU Manager Policies via Options

The static CPU policy accepts a `CPUManagerPolicyOptions` map passed at kubelet startup. Options such as `full-pcpus-only`, `distribute-cpus-across-numa`, and `prefer-align-cpus-by-uncore-cache` alter the `cpuAccumulator` allocation strategy in `cpu_assignment.go` without requiring a different policy name. Adding a new option only requires implementing a new code path inside `policy_static.go:Allocate` and declaring the option in the feature-gated options registry.

**Risk**: Options interact. `distribute-cpus-across-numa` and `full-pcpus-only` together may produce no valid allocation on nodes with odd CPU counts, causing guaranteed pods to be perpetually rejected. There is no static validation of option combinations; failures surface only at runtime as admission errors.

### 5.2 Topology Manager Policies

Four policy implementations exist in `pkg/kubelet/cm/topologymanager/`: `none`, `best-effort`, `restricted`, `single-numa-node`. They share the `HintMerger` in `policy.go` but differ in how they handle non-preferred hints. New policies can be added by implementing the `Policy` interface (`Name() string; Merge(logger, providersHints) (TopologyHint, bool)`) and registering in `NewManager`.

**Risk**: A custom policy that always returns `preferred=true` for any affinity mask will never reject a pod, silently undermining NUMA locality guarantees without surfacing errors.

### 5.3 Topology Manager Scopes

Two scopes exist (`scope_container.go`, `scope_pod.go`). Pod scope aggregates hints across all containers before merging; container scope merges per-container independently. Switching from container to pod scope on a running node with allocated Guaranteed pods requires draining the node, because pod scope may accept pods that container scope would reject (or vice versa).

### 5.4 Device Plugins

Third-party device plugins implement the v1beta1 gRPC API in `pkg/kubelet/cm/devicemanager/plugin/v1beta1/`. Plugins must implement `ListAndWatch`, `Allocate`, and optionally `GetTopologyHints`. The device manager calls `GetTopologyHints` during the topology manager's admission phase; a plugin that returns inaccurate NUMA topology hints will cause the topology manager to make suboptimal placements without any error signal.

Plugins that return non-reproducible allocations across `Allocate` calls break the checkpoint restore invariant: after kubelet restart, the checkpoint may contain a device ID that the plugin no longer knows about.

### 5.5 `HintProvider` Registration

`cm.topologyManager.AddHintProvider(logger, provider)` in `container_manager_linux.go:312,341,356` is called during construction. This is not a runtime extension point; there is no way to register a new hint provider after `NewContainerManager` returns. In-tree managers are the only registered providers today.

### 5.6 `InternalContainerLifecycle` Hooks

`PreCreateContainer` in `internal_container_lifecycle_linux.go` is the place where CRI container config is modified (e.g., to inject CPU affinity annotations). Adding a new resource type that needs CRI-level config injection requires extending this hook. The interface is internal (`package cm`) and not a stable extension point for external components.

---

## 6. Operational Implications

### 6.1 Performance

**Admission latency**: Every pod admission call traverses the topology manager, which in turn calls `GetTopologyHints` on all registered providers synchronously. For a cluster with 3 resource managers and up to 8 NUMA nodes, the worst-case hint permutation count is O((2^8)^3) ≈ 16M. In practice, constraints reduce this by orders of magnitude, but nodes with many NUMA nodes and many resource types will exhibit measurable admission latency. Monitor `kubelet_topology_manager_admission_duration_ms` (emitted via `metrics`) to detect regressions.

**CPU reconcile loop overhead**: The reconcile loop calls `runtimeService.UpdateContainerResources()` for every container whose CPU assignment differs from the last recorded state. On a node with hundreds of containers, the loop may generate significant CRI traffic every `CPUManagerReconcilePeriod`. Widen the period (`--cpu-manager-reconcile-period`) to reduce CRI overhead if CPU affinity changes are infrequent in your workload.

**QoS cgroup update interval**: `qosContainerManager` recomputes burstable and best-effort cgroup limits every minute. On a busy node with rapidly changing pod counts, this update may arrive 60 s after a pod terminates, leaving residual memory headroom unclaimed. This is acceptable for typical workloads but can cause transient OOM pressure immediately after high-density pod churn.

### 6.2 Reliability

**Kubelet restart recovery**: CPU and memory managers restore state from checkpoint before `sourcesReady` is declared. Until sources are ready, stale state removal is suppressed (`reconcileState` skips removal). This window ensures that containers started by a prior kubelet invocation are not deprived of their CPU affinity during the brief period when pod sources are still being synchronized.

**Device plugin disconnection**: The device manager uses gRPC streaming (`ListAndWatch`) to receive device health updates. If the plugin crashes or disconnects, its devices are immediately marked unhealthy in the manager's internal map. Capacity reported via `GetDevicePluginResourceCapacity` drops to zero for that resource, preventing new pods from being scheduled to the node. Running pods retain their device files — the kernel does not revoke them — but the plugin is no longer available to handle `Allocate` for new containers.

**cgroup write failures**: `enforceNodeAllocatableCgroups` retries memory limit writes indefinitely when running pods' memory exceeds the target limit. This is the intended behavior: forcibly setting a lower limit would immediately OOM-kill running workloads. However, if eviction is misconfigured or stalled, the node will emit repeated `FailedNodeAllocatableEnforcement` events without ever converging. Operators should monitor for this event as a signal that eviction thresholds need tuning.

### 6.3 Maintainability

**Platform divergence**: `containerManagerImpl` has separate concrete implementations under build tags `linux`, `windows`, and the stub (`container_manager_stub.go`). The Windows implementation (`container_manager_windows.go`) contains a minimal struct with `deviceManager`, `topologyManager`, `cpuManager`, and `memoryManager` fields, but cgroup enforcement and QoS hierarchy are absent. Any change to `ContainerManager` interface in `container_manager.go` must be reflected in all three platform implementations. The mock in `pkg/kubelet/cm/testing/mocks.go` is auto-generated from the interface and must be regenerated after interface changes (`//go:generate mockery` in `container_manager.go:17`).

**State schema evolution**: Checkpoint files embed a policy name. If the policy name changes (e.g., a rename of `"static"` to `"pinned"`), kubelet will consider the checkpoint invalid and reset all allocations at startup. This means all Guaranteed pods must be restarted to regain exclusive CPU/memory pinning after such a policy rename.

---

## 7. File and Directory Map

| Path | Role |
|---|---|
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/container_manager.go` | `ContainerManager` interface definition; `NodeConfig`, `NodeAllocatableConfig`, `Status` types |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/container_manager_linux.go` | Linux `containerManagerImpl`: construction, `Start()`, wiring of sub-managers |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/container_manager_windows.go` | Windows stub: minimal `containerManagerImpl`, no cgroup hierarchy |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/container_manager_stub.go` | Unsupported-platform stub |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/types.go` | `CgroupManager`, `PodContainerManager`, `CgroupName`, `ResourceConfig`, `CgroupConfig` interfaces and types |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/internal_container_lifecycle.go` | `InternalContainerLifecycle` interface; `internalContainerLifecycleImpl` implementation |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/internal_container_lifecycle_linux.go` | Linux-specific `PreCreateContainer` (CRI config injection) |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/cgroup_manager_linux.go` | `CgroupName.ToSystemd()`, name escaping, `NewCgroupManager` dispatch |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/cgroup_v1_manager_linux.go` | cgroupfs v1 CRUD operations |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/cgroup_v2_manager_linux.go` | cgroupfs v2 unified hierarchy CRUD operations |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/qos_container_manager_linux.go` | `QOSContainerManager`: Guaranteed/Burstable/BestEffort cgroup management |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/node_container_manager_linux.go` | Node allocatable cgroup creation and enforcement with retry logic |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/pod_container_manager_linux.go` | `PodContainerManager`: per-pod cgroup lifecycle |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/cpumanager/cpu_manager.go` | CPU `Manager` interface; `manager` struct; reconcile loop |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/cpumanager/policy_static.go` | Static CPU policy: `Allocate`, `allocateCPUs`, NUMA-aware assignment |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/cpumanager/cpu_assignment.go` | `cpuAccumulator`: topology-aware CPU selection algorithm |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/cpumanager/state/state_checkpoint.go` | Checkpoint-backed state persistence |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/memorymanager/memory_manager.go` | Memory `Manager` interface and implementation |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/memorymanager/policy_static.go` | Static memory policy: NUMA-pinned allocation |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/topologymanager/topology_manager.go` | `Manager` and `HintProvider` interfaces; `TopologyAffinityError`; NUMA node limit |
